title: Whether a city's transit map should show wealth inequality
summary: Argue whether public transit maps should overlay neighbourhood wealth so that riders can see whom the network actually serves, or whether such maps cross a line into political design.
topic: Whether transit maps should overlay wealth inequality data
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether a city's official public transit map (London Tube map, São Paulo metro map, Hanoi BRT map) should be redesigned to overlay neighbourhood wealth — colouring stations by median income, or shading the network thinner where it underserves poorer districts — so riders can see whom the network actually serves. Should the transit authority do this, or does this cross a line from civic information into political design? Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted,'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Take a clear position. Avoid both populist and technocratic clichés.
hintStarters: Looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes, you notice that the network's apparent…
What an official transit map quietly does, beyond the obvious work of telling riders how to change at the next interchange, is something cartographers know well and the public is rarely…
Granted, the transit authority is in the business of moving people from A to B, not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding, and the…

hintVocab.meaningVi: bản đồ giao thông công cộng
khu vực được phục vụ kém
khoảng cách mile đầu / mile cuối
thiết kế thông tin trung lập
thiết kế mang tính chính trị
làm cho bất bình đẳng dễ đọc
bản đồ như công cụ chính trị

sampleText:
Looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes, you notice that the network's apparent fairness — every station equidistant on the diagram, every line equally weighted in colour — disappears the moment income is added as a layer. The Central line, drawn as a single uniform red, suddenly looks like two networks running through different countries. Stations in the east are barely visible. Stations in the west glow. Nothing about the schematic itself was lying. It simply was not telling the whole truth.

What an official transit map quietly does, beyond the obvious work of telling riders how to change at the next interchange, is something cartographers know well and the public is rarely invited to consider. By flattening the city's distances, by giving every line the same weight, and by leaving income out of the picture entirely, the map presents a network that appears neutral when it is, in fact, the product of decades of political choice about whom the network was built for. The map does not invent inequality. It politely declines to render it.

Granted, the transit authority is in the business of moving people from A to B, not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding, and the introduction of income shading risks turning the daily commute into a confrontation with statistics that most riders have not asked for. There is also a genuine craft argument: every layer added to a transit map degrades its core function of helping someone in a hurry find the right train. A map that does everything tends to do nothing well.

Even so, the claim that the current map is neutral cannot survive even a brief inspection of what it omits. A schematic that draws a five-minute walk and a fifteen-minute walk as if they were the same distance, and that gives a station serving thirty thousand riders the same dot as one serving three thousand, has already made an enormous political choice. It has chosen not to show what is unequal. The right comparison is not between a neutral map and a political one. It is between two different politics — the politics of legibility and the politics of pleasant invisibility.

The defensible position is not that every Tube map should look like a heatmap of household income. It is that the transit authority should publish an official equity-overlay version of the map alongside the schematic one, that the equity version should be the default on the authority's website, and that the schematic should carry a small line acknowledging what it does not show. The point is not to politicise the map. It is to stop pretending that the existing one is not already political.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes, you notice that the network's apparent fairness — every station equidistant on the diagram, every line equally weighted in colour — disappears the moment income is added as a layer
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Nhìn vào bản đồ tàu điện ngầm London chính thức bên cạnh một lớp phủ được tạo bởi một nhà nghiên cứu quy hoạch tô màu các tuyến theo thu nhập hộ gia đình dọc theo đường đi của chúng, bạn nhận ra rằng sự công bằng bề ngoài của mạng lưới — mỗi ga cách đều nhau trên sơ đồ, mỗi tuyến có cùng trọng số màu sắc — biến mất ngay khi thu nhập được thêm vào như một lớp

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The Central line, drawn as a single uniform red, suddenly looks like two networks running through different countries
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Tuyến Central, được vẽ như một màu đỏ đồng nhất, đột nhiên trông giống như hai mạng lưới chạy qua hai quốc gia khác nhau

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Stations in the east are barely visible. Stations in the west glow

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Nothing about the schematic itself was lying. It simply was not telling the whole truth

sampleAnnotations.phrase: By flattening the city's distances, by giving every line the same weight, and by leaving income out of the picture entirely, the map presents a network that appears neutral when it is, in fact, the product of decades of political choice about whom the network was built for

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Bản đồ không phát minh ra bất bình đẳng. Nó lịch sự từ chối hiển thị nó

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the transit authority is in the business of moving people from A to B, not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding

sampleAnnotations.phrase: It has chosen not to show what is unequal
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Nó đã chọn không hiển thị cái gì là không bình đẳng

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The right comparison is not between a neutral map and a political one. It is between two different politics — the politics of legibility and the politics of pleasant invisibility
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: So sánh đúng đắn không phải là giữa một bản đồ trung lập và một bản đồ chính trị. Đó là giữa hai chính trị khác nhau — chính trị của tính dễ đọc và chính trị của sự vô hình dễ chịu

sampleAnnotations.phrase: the transit authority should publish an official equity-overlay version of the map alongside the schematic one, that the equity version should be the default on the authority's website, and that the schematic should carry a small line acknowledging what it does not show
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: cơ quan giao thông nên xuất bản một phiên bản chính thức của bản đồ có lớp phủ công bằng bên cạnh phiên bản sơ đồ, rằng phiên bản công bằng nên là mặc định trên trang web của cơ quan, và rằng sơ đồ nên mang một dòng nhỏ thừa nhận những gì nó không hiển thị

sampleAnnotations.phrase: The point is not to politicise the map. It is to stop pretending that the existing one is not already political
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: Quan điểm không phải là chính trị hóa bản đồ. Đó là ngừng giả vờ rằng bản đồ hiện hữu không đã mang tính chính trị
làm cho bất bình đẳng dễ đọc

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft + 'beyond the obvious work' to expose hidden function
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What an official transit map quietly does, beyond the obvious work of telling riders how to change at the next interchange, is something cartographers know well and the public is rarely invited to consider' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' kết hợp với cụm chèn 'beyond the obvious work of…' để loại bỏ chức năng bề mặt trước khi đưa ra chức năng ẩn. Trạng từ 'quietly' báo hiệu rằng bài luận sẽ vạch trần điều bản đồ đang làm mà không khoa trương.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What an official transit map quietly does, beyond the obvious work of telling riders how to change at the next interchange, is something cartographers know well and the public is rarely invited to consider' combines a what-cleft with an inserted 'beyond the obvious work of…' phrase to rule out the surface function before introducing the hidden one. The adverb 'quietly' flags that the essay will expose what the map is doing without fanfare.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive 'is rarely invited to consider' + 'is rendered' for institutional erasure
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'the public is rarely invited to consider' và 'It politely declines to render it' (lưu ý từ 'render' ở thể chủ động ám chỉ một quyết định) dùng passive và các động từ trung tính để gán trách nhiệm cho thiết kế chính thức mà không cần đặt tên ai. Cấu trúc 'is rarely invited' đặc biệt hiệu quả — nó vạch trần sự loại trừ bằng cách mô tả nó như một sự bỏ qua thay vì một sự cấm đoán.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'the public is rarely invited to consider' and 'It politely declines to render it' (note the active 'render' implying a decision) use the passive and politely neutral verbs to assign responsibility to official design without naming any person. 'Is rarely invited' is especially effective — it exposes exclusion by describing it as an omission rather than a prohibition.

sampleTranslationVi:
Nhìn vào bản đồ tàu điện ngầm London chính thức bên cạnh một lớp phủ được tạo bởi một nhà nghiên cứu quy hoạch tô màu các tuyến theo thu nhập hộ gia đình dọc theo đường đi của chúng, bạn nhận ra rằng sự công bằng bề ngoài của mạng lưới — mỗi ga cách đều nhau trên sơ đồ, mỗi tuyến có cùng trọng số màu sắc — biến mất ngay khi thu nhập được thêm vào như một lớp. Tuyến Central, được vẽ như một màu đỏ đồng nhất, đột nhiên trông giống như hai mạng lưới chạy qua hai quốc gia khác nhau. Các ga ở phía đông hầu như không nhìn thấy. Các ga ở phía tây thì sáng rực. Không có gì về sơ đồ tự nó đang nói dối. Nó chỉ đơn giản là không nói toàn bộ sự thật.

Điều mà một bản đồ giao thông chính thức lặng lẽ làm, vượt ra ngoài công việc hiển nhiên là nói cho hành khách cách chuyển tuyến ở ga chuyển tiếp tiếp theo, là điều mà các nhà bản đồ học biết rõ và công chúng hiếm khi được mời cân nhắc. Bằng cách làm phẳng các khoảng cách của thành phố, bằng cách cho mọi tuyến cùng trọng số, và bằng cách hoàn toàn để thu nhập ra ngoài bức tranh, bản đồ trình bày một mạng lưới có vẻ trung lập trong khi nó thực sự là sản phẩm của hàng thập kỷ lựa chọn chính trị về việc mạng lưới được xây dựng cho ai. Bản đồ không phát minh ra bất bình đẳng. Nó lịch sự từ chối hiển thị nó.

Công nhận, cơ quan giao thông là trong việc kinh doanh di chuyển người từ A đến B, không phải sản xuất các tờ rơi chính trị ngụy trang thành định hướng đường đi, và việc giới thiệu tô màu thu nhập có nguy cơ biến chuyến đi làm hàng ngày thành một cuộc đối đầu với thống kê mà hầu hết hành khách không yêu cầu. Cũng có một lập luận về thủ công thực sự: mỗi lớp được thêm vào một bản đồ giao thông làm suy giảm chức năng cốt lõi của nó là giúp một người đang vội tìm đúng chuyến tàu. Một bản đồ làm mọi thứ có xu hướng không làm gì tốt.

Dẫu vậy, tuyên bố rằng bản đồ hiện tại là trung lập không thể sống sót ngay cả một cuộc kiểm tra ngắn về những gì nó bỏ qua. Một sơ đồ vẽ một quãng đi bộ năm phút và một quãng đi bộ mười lăm phút như thể chúng cùng khoảng cách, và cho một ga phục vụ ba mươi nghìn hành khách cùng một chấm như một ga phục vụ ba nghìn, đã đưa ra một lựa chọn chính trị khổng lồ. Nó đã chọn không hiển thị cái gì là không bình đẳng. So sánh đúng đắn không phải là giữa một bản đồ trung lập và một bản đồ chính trị. Đó là giữa hai chính trị khác nhau — chính trị của tính dễ đọc và chính trị của sự vô hình dễ chịu.

Lập trường có thể bảo vệ được không phải là mỗi bản đồ tàu điện ngầm nên trông giống như một bản đồ nhiệt của thu nhập hộ gia đình. Đó là cơ quan giao thông nên xuất bản một phiên bản chính thức của bản đồ có lớp phủ công bằng bên cạnh phiên bản sơ đồ, rằng phiên bản công bằng nên là mặc định trên trang web của cơ quan, và rằng sơ đồ nên mang một dòng nhỏ thừa nhận những gì nó không hiển thị. Quan điểm không phải là chính trị hóa bản đồ. Đó là ngừng giả vờ rằng bản đồ hiện hữu không đã mang tính chính trị.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to expose what an official transit map quietly does?
options: What an official transit map quietly does, beyond the obvious work of telling riders how to change at the next interchange, is something cartographers know well and the public is rarely invited to consider.
Cartographers know well the public is rarely invited to consider what an official transit map quietly does beyond the obvious work.
Telling riders how to change at the next interchange is the obvious work beyond what an official transit map quietly does.
Quietly does what the public is rarely invited to consider an official transit map cartographers know well beyond the obvious work.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' with an inserted 'beyond the obvious work of…' phrase rules out the surface function before delivering the hidden one.

options: To make inequality legal
To make inequality visible and readable in a public document — turning a structural pattern into something a reader can see at a glance
To write a book about inequality
To make legible handwriting unequal
mcQuestions.explanation: Legibilising is the design act of making something readable. The essay argues that overlaying income on a transit map legibilises inequality that the standard map politely hides.
mcQuestions.hint: What does it mean to make something 'legible' as a document?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted,' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, the transit authority is in the business of moving people from A to B, not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding.
The transit authority granted is in the business of moving people from A to B not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding.
Producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding granted the transit authority is in the business of moving people from A to B not of.
Moving people granted from A to B is in the business the transit authority not of producing political pamphlets disguised as wayfinding.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause is the cleanest B2 concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction places the public in subject position to background the cartographers?
options: The public is rarely invited to consider what an official transit map quietly does beyond the obvious work.
Rarely invited an official transit map quietly does the public to consider what beyond the obvious work.
Beyond the obvious work to consider the public an official transit map quietly does rarely invited what.
An official transit map the public quietly does what beyond the obvious work is rarely invited to consider.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is rarely invited' is a passive construction that puts the public in subject position and hides whoever is doing — or, in this case, not doing — the inviting.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is rarely invited' as a passive?

options: A desert region with no railways
An area of a city where transit service is so sparse — long waits, distant stations, few routes — that residents are effectively cut off from the network
A restaurant with bad service
A district that does not allow military service
mcQuestions.explanation: Service deserts are the structural gaps in transit provision that the standard schematic map renders invisible — and the essay argues an overlay would show them clearly.
mcQuestions.hint: What is a 'desert' in food terms (a 'food desert'), and apply that to transit service.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a participle clause to set the analytical scene?
options: Looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes, you notice that the network's apparent fairness disappears.

You notice looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes that the network's apparent fairness disappears.

The network's apparent fairness disappears looking at the official London Tube map you notice next to an overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes.

Along their routes by household income shades the lines an overlay produced by a planning researcher looking at the official London Tube map you notice that the network's apparent fairness disappears next to.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Looking at the official London Tube map next to an overlay…' is a present-participle clause opener that sets the analytical scene before the main clause delivers the observation.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for an -ing phrase at the start of the sentence, separated by a comma from the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example, all maps lie.
Even so, the claim that the current map is neutral cannot survive even a brief inspection of what it omits.
On the contrary, no map has ever shown anything.
Furthermore, all schematics are perfect.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the previous concession (the wayfinding craft argument is real) and pivots back to the writer's main argument by attacking the central premise: that the existing map is neutral.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's broader position?

options: Owning shares in a transit company
Fair distribution of transit access and service quality across neighbourhoods of different incomes
Equity loans for transit construction
Equal weight given to all transit maps
mcQuestions.explanation: Transit equity is the principle that transit access should not be unequally distributed by income; the essay argues that current maps quietly hide failures of equity.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'equity' mean in policy terms?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly, the Tube map is just basically lying to everyone, no question.
It's kind of obvious that maps are political, full stop.

The schematic conventions of contemporary transit cartography — equidistant station spacing, uniform line weighting, absence of demographic overlay — embed political choices that the resulting maps then present as neutral information.
Maps are like, you know, supposed to show inequality and stuff.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('schematic conventions', 'demographic overlay', 'embed political choices') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal markers.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: Transit maps should be redesigned every year to reflect new political slogans.
Transit authorities should keep using only the existing schematic and refuse to show any inequality data.

Transit authorities should publish an official equity-overlay map alongside the schematic, make the equity version the default on their website, and acknowledge on the schematic itself what it does not show — because the current schematic is already political, just invisibly so.
All transit maps should be banned and replaced with text directions.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay rejects both pure technocracy and pure activism in favour of an honest dual-publication policy.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's specific dual-map proposal.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'the politics of pleasant invisibility'?
options: The right to remain politically silent
The deliberately uncontroversial design choice of leaving inequality off the map — politically pleasant precisely because it spares both rider and authority an uncomfortable conversation
Invisible political parties
The pleasant feeling of being unable to see anything
mcQuestions.explanation: Pleasant invisibility is the politics of the polished schematic — it offers comfort by not showing what would be uncomfortable to see.
mcQuestions.hint: What makes invisibility 'pleasant', and for whom?

options: The first and last miles of a marathon
The distance between a rider's origin (or destination) and the nearest transit stop — often longest in underserved areas, even when the transit network looks dense on the map
The first and last carriages on a train
The first mile of new track laid each year
mcQuestions.explanation: The first-mile/last-mile gap is the walking or connecting distance to and from transit — invisible on schematic maps but heavily unequal across neighbourhoods.
mcQuestions.hint: What is the gap between your house and the nearest station, and why does that matter?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a defining relative clause correctly?
options: An overlay produced by a planning researcher that shades the lines by household income along their routes makes the inequality visible.
By household income along their routes shades the lines an overlay produced by a planning researcher that makes the inequality visible.
Inequality visible by household income makes an overlay produced by a planning researcher along their routes that shades the lines.
Along their routes that shades the lines makes the inequality visible an overlay produced by a planning researcher by household income.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'that shades the lines by household income along their routes' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which overlay the writer means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to flatten distance' mean in cartography?
options: To physically flatten paper maps
To represent unequal real-world distances as equal in a schematic diagram — for readability — at the cost of geographic accuracy
To shorten all distances on a journey
To remove all mountains from a map
mcQuestions.explanation: Flattening distance is the central trick of transit schematics — and the essay argues that this readability comes at the price of hiding which neighbourhoods are actually well-served.
mcQuestions.hint: What happens to real-world distances on a Tube map?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence closes the essay with a sharp, summary verdict?
options: In conclusion, transit maps are quite complicated.
Therefore, all maps must include political data.
The point is not to politicise the map. It is to stop pretending that the existing one is not already political.
To sum up, both kinds of maps have advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C closes with a sharp redefinition: the question is not whether to politicise but whether to be honest about the politics already there.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a sharp 'not X. It is Y.' redefinition?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what is the strongest evidence that the current Tube map is already political?
options: That it is printed in colour rather than black and white

That it represents a five-minute walk and a fifteen-minute walk as the same distance, and gives a station serving thirty thousand riders the same dot as one serving three thousand — choices that hide enormous real-world inequalities
That it includes only the Tube, not the buses
That it is sometimes changed by the authority
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph four names this exact diagnosis: the existing schematic has already chosen what to hide.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph four for the writer's specific evidence.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of the transit map for a city you have used regularly. What does it hide about which neighbourhoods are well-served and which are not? If you were designing the next version, would you add an income overlay, a frequency overlay, or something else — and what would you be willing to make less readable in exchange?
